meta.notes: Novel AI-generated PSBB practice questions for the SpeedyCAT proof-of-concept. NOT real MCAT/AAMC content. Text-only. Chunk 7 of 10.

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the intangible ideas, values, beliefs, and norms shared within a society?

choices.label: A
choices.text: secondary deviance

choices.label: B
choices.text: multiculturalism

choices.label: C
choices.text: nonmaterial culture

choices.label: D
choices.text: cultural lag

questions.explanation: This description matches nonmaterial culture, which is the intangible ideas, values, beliefs, and norms shared within a society. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes cultural lag?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patterns or traits found in all known human cultures.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An initial norm violation that has little effect on a person's self-concept.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The delay between a change in material culture and the adjustment of nonmaterial culture to it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A norm so deeply held that its violation provokes extreme disgust or revulsion.

questions.explanation: Cultural lag is best described as the delay between a change in material culture and the adjustment of nonmaterial culture to it. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of a counterculture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A rural region developing factories, cities, and bureaucratic institutions over decades.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A movement that rejects mainstream consumerism and lives communally off the grid.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hit songs, blockbuster films, and viral videos enjoyed by the general public.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A formerly incarcerated person being treated first and foremost as an ex-convict.

questions.explanation: An example of a counterculture is: a movement that rejects mainstream consumerism and lives communally off the grid. In general, a counterculture refers to a group whose norms and values actively oppose or reject those of the dominant culture. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: informal everyday norms whose violation brings mild disapproval rather than serious punishment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: folkways

choices.label: B
choices.text: nonmaterial culture

choices.label: C
choices.text: modernization

choices.label: D
choices.text: cultural universals

questions.explanation: This description matches folkways, which is informal everyday norms whose violation brings mild disapproval rather than serious punishment. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes sanctions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A norm so deeply held that its violation provokes extreme disgust or revulsion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A group within a society that shares distinctive norms and values while sharing the larger culture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tendency to evaluate other cultures by the standards of one's own culture, seen as superior.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The rewards and punishments a group uses to enforce conformity to its norms.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of stigma?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A formerly incarcerated person being treated first and foremost as an ex-convict.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sweeping public alarm that a new video game is corrupting the nation's youth.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hit songs, blockbuster films, and viral videos enjoyed by the general public.

questions.explanation: An example of stigma is: a formerly incarcerated person being treated first and foremost as an ex-convict. In general, stigma refers to a deeply negative label that reshapes a person's self-concept and social identity. The other options illustrate different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the absolute threshold?

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes social stratification?

choices.text: Defending a choice by appeal to universal justice over a written law.

questions.explanation: An example of social loafing is: one member coasting while others carry a group project. In general, social loafing refers to the tendency to exert less effort when working as part of a group. The other options illustrate different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the self-fulfilling prophecy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A teacher's high expectations leading a student to actually perform better.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An anonymous person in a mob acting far more aggressively than usual.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A school-age child grasping that pouring juice into a new glass keeps the amount the same.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blaming your own lateness on traffic but a colleague's on laziness.

questions.explanation: An example of the self-fulfilling prophecy is: a teacher's high expectations leading a student to actually perform better. In general, the self-fulfilling prophecy refers to a belief that brings about its own fulfillment through changed behavior. The other options illustrate different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the Yerkes-Dodson law?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Seeking out a thrilling activity when life feels boring and understimulating.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A hormone that rises under stress and raises available blood sugar.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The draining pressure of demands that feel beyond one's control.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Test performance being best at moderate anxiety and worse when calm or panicked.

questions.explanation: An example of the Yerkes-Dodson law is: test performance being best at moderate anxiety and worse when calm or panicked. In general, the Yerkes-Dodson law refers to the principle that performance peaks at a moderate level of arousal. The other options illustrate different concepts.
